title: Today's Tech Brief: AI's Confidence Problem and a Microsoft Feud
summary: A look at the latest tech news, including a feud between Microsoft and a 0-day hunter, and AI's persistent accuracy issues.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's tech brief. We're looking at a couple of interesting stories making waves in the tech world. First up, a 0-day hunter is making headlines with a very public feud with Microsoft. This researcher, who has been sharing vulnerabilities with the company, claims they've been 'humiliated' by Microsoft's response and is threatening to release another 'bone-shattering drop' of exploits on July 14. Microsoft, for their part, has called in law enforcement. It's a tense situation that highlights the often-cat-and-mouse game between security researchers and tech giants.

The stakes are high here, as this researcher claims to have found six 0-day vulnerabilities, with three already under active exploitation. That's a lot of potential trouble for Windows users. I'm curious to see how this plays out – will Microsoft be able to resolve the situation without any more damage, or will we see a significant security incident?

Next, a report from Axios notes that AI systems are getting things wrong more confidently than ever. It's a bit like that friend who always seems to be certain they're right, even when they're not. The issue here is that as AI becomes more integrated into our daily lives, its accuracy – or lack thereof – has significant implications. I'm looking at this and wondering how these systems can improve, or at least, become more transparent about their limitations.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.

sources.title: Disgruntled 0-day hunter 'humiliated' by Microsoft pledges 'bone shattering drop' as Redmond calls cops
AI is still getting things wrong, more confidently than ever - Axios
